brand.name: Brooklyn Spectacles
brand.vertical: Local & service — independent eyewear retail (DTC + brick-and-mortar)

summary: Brooklyn Spectacles is an independent eyewear brand that designs and hand-finishes optical frames, sunglasses, and a children's line ('Little Spectacles') in Italian acetate at its Williamsburg, Brooklyn studio. It sells direct-to-consumer online and at its flagship at 291 Bedford Ave, plus through authorized US and Canada retailers, serving style-conscious urban creatives and professionals who want distinctive, well-made frames over mass-market chain eyewear. Purchases are one-off (roughly $195–$225 per frame) with a natural 1–3 year repeat cycle driven by prescription changes, frame wear, or wanting a new look.

campaigns.name: Welcome & Onboarding
campaigns.objective: Bridge the gap between checkout and the unboxing 'aha' — reinforce craftsmanship, set care expectations, and seed the brand relationship so a one-time buyer has reasons to return.
campaigns.trigger: Purchase confirmed (Shopify order created); send email 1 within seconds of checkout.
campaigns.segment: New buyers (online DTC); branch by product line — optical, sunglasses, or Little Spectacles (kids).
campaigns.rationale: Buyers currently get only a bare Shopify order confirmation, yet this is the highest-engagement moment to justify a premium price, prevent buyer's remorse before delivery, and plant the care + second-pair relationship that fights the brand's single-purchase mentality.

emails.purpose: Instant order confirmation reframed as a craft story — confirm the purchase, set the delivery timeline, reinforce why the price is worth it, and build anticipation for the unboxing.
emails.subject: Your frames are being handmade in Williamsburg
emails.preview: Here's what's happening to your Italian-acetate frames right now — and what to expect when the box lands.
emails.cta: See how your frames are made
keyPoints: Confirm the order and set a clear delivery timeline so there's no anxiety during the wait
Reinforce the craft — Italian acetate, hand-finishing, real metal hinges — the reasons it cost more than a chain pair
Build anticipation for the 'aha' unboxing moment and invite a #LoveMyBKS share once they're wearing them
Lead with simple care basics and a real reply-to address so a person is on the other end

campaigns.name: Lead-Magnet Nurture
campaigns.objective: Convert email subscribers who have browsed but not bought into first-time customers by dismantling the two blockers — price and online-fit anxiety — without discounting.
campaigns.trigger: Email-list signup (footer) with no purchase; lead has not converted within the browse window.
campaigns.segment: Non-buyer subscribers; branch by browsing interest — optical, sunglasses, or kids — where known.
campaigns.rationale: The footer signup exists with no nurture behind it. For this audience the stall is specific: price ($195–$225) and 'will they suit my face / fit my prescription online,' so the nurture's job is to retire those exact objections rather than chase a coupon.

emails.purpose: Retire the price and fit objections head-on — justify the cost through longevity and craft, and remove online-purchase risk with the fit and return guarantee.
emails.subject: Worth it? The math on a pair that lasts years
emails.preview: Italian acetate, real hinges, and a no-risk fit guarantee — why $195 beats replacing cheap frames twice.
keyPoints: Cost-per-wear math: one $195 handmade pair outlasts several warping fast-fashion frames
Address fit anxiety directly — measurements, face-shape guidance, and an easy return/exchange policy take the risk off online buying
Contrast with the mass-market chains the audience is tired of (the Warby Parker / LensCrafters look-alike problem)
Single CTA into the collection; invite a reply with a face-shape question to a real stylist

campaigns.name: Renewal, Prescription Anniversary & Expansion
campaigns.objective: Manufacture the repeat-purchase trigger this single-purchase business lacks — reactivate past buyers at their natural prescription-refresh moment and drive second-pair (sun + optical) expansion.
campaigns.trigger: Purchase-date anniversary (~18–24 months post-purchase for a prescription refresh; ~60–90 days for a second-pair add-on).
campaigns.segment: Past buyers; branch by health — recent/engaged buyers get the second-pair expansion offer, long-lapsed buyers get the prescription-refresh nudge.
campaigns.rationale: With no subscription and a 1–3 year repeat cycle, the brand's single biggest revenue lever is creating a reason to return at the moment a prescription typically changes — otherwise a satisfied customer silently buys their next pair from a chain.

emails.purpose: Reach past buyers around the 18-month mark — when a prescription often shifts — and turn a dormant relationship into a new-pair purchase.
emails.subject: Still getting compliments two years on?
emails.preview: Prescriptions drift and styles move — here's what we've made since your last pair.
keyPoints: Frame it around the natural prescription cycle (commonly shifts every 1–2 years) — a non-pushy reason to refresh
Showcase new street-named frames and collaboration drops released since their last purchase
Offer both paths: an in-store fitting at 291 Bedford Ave or online with the fit guarantee
Recognize returning customers with loyalty acknowledgment, not a discount race

campaigns.name: Win-Back / Re-Engagement
campaigns.objective: Reactivate dormant subscribers and lapsed buyers with fresh brand momentum, then sunset the truly disengaged to protect deliverability.
campaigns.trigger: 60–90 days of no opens/clicks (dormant subscriber), or a long gap since last purchase with no engagement (lapsed buyer).
campaigns.segment: Dormant subscribers and lapsed buyers, messaged separately — lapsed buyers reference what they bought, dormant subscribers lean on fresh value.
campaigns.rationale: The single-purchase mentality means satisfied customers go quiet for years; a win-back recovers 5–15% of them with new collections and a light gesture before they default to a competitor at their next prescription change.

emails.purpose: Re-open the relationship with what's new in Brooklyn since they went quiet, plus a small welcome-back gesture — the highest-recovery send in the flow.
emails.subject: It's been a while — Brooklyn's been busy
emails.preview: New frames, new collabs, and a little something to welcome you back.
keyPoints: Pattern-interrupt with fresh value: new collections and collaboration drops since they last engaged
A modest, on-brand gesture (free lens-care kit or store credit toward a second pair) rather than a deep discount
Lapsed-buyer branch references their original pair; dormant-subscriber branch leans on new arrivals
Set up the sunset path — non-responders flow to a final 'should we stop emailing?' before suppression

campaigns.name: Value Newsletter — Dispatches from the Studio
campaigns.objective: Stay top-of-mind across the multi-year gap between purchases by building brand authority and Brooklyn-culture affinity — without discounting.
campaigns.segment: All engaged contacts; suppress contacts in active onboarding (Campaign 1) and route recently-dormant contacts to Win-Back (Campaign 4) instead.
campaigns.rationale: Because a buyer has no functional reason to return for 1–3 years, an always-on editorial keeps the brand present so that when the prescription does change, Brooklyn Spectacles is the default choice — not a chain.

emails.purpose: The flagship recurring edition that sets the format — one craft/culture story, one styling tip, and a soft look at what's new — building a monthly reading habit.
emails.subject: The street behind the frame: this month from the studio
emails.preview: A frame's origin story, one face-shape styling tip, and what we're making next.
keyPoints: Repeatable 80/20 format: a Williamsburg craft or street-name origin story plus one practical styling/fit tip
Builds habit with a recurring section readers return for; a single primary CTA per send
Soft, non-pushy product moment (a new arrival or collab) instead of a sale
Invite replies and #LoveMyBKS UGC to keep the community loop alive
